speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Pauline Opat, and I'm interviewing Lisa or Elizabeth Etinger on the 11th of October, 1996, in Melbourne. OK. Can you start by telling us your name, your date of birth, and your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm-- I born 10th of June, 1922 in Vinnitsa. I am now 75 years old. I mean 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is [INAUDIBLE] Beri.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-I-R-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: What city? In Vinnitsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What large-- what large city is near Vinnitsa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vinnitsa is a large city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other city is near. It's Ukraine. And it's not far from Kiev. In what see if you did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grow up in Vinnitsa till I was about nine years of age, and then my father was an army officer. So And they sent him north to Leningrad, where we lived up to 1938-- '39. And-- yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- Can you tell me the names of your parents and your siblings and their dates of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother is maiden name was Gefter. She married my father, Beri David-- David. My mother was born and Vinnitsa too. My father was born in a little town, Mariupol. It's all in Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their date of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the date of death--

speaker: interviewer
text: The year--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of birth of them, but do-- I do remember when they died. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sisters. The middle's-- I'm the eldest. My middle sister is [? Raissa-- ?] like Rachel. And my youngest sister is Alexsandra, [? Shura ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- my middle sister born in 1920-- '24 in December. And my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] youngest sister born in 1928 in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your last address before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My last address before the war was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. We lived in blocks of flats for Army officers, which we called [RUSSIAN]. It's in Russian. I wouldn't know how to translate it in-- in German. It's 100 flats, approximately, and they was occupied only by Army officers, Red Army officers. That was my last place where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's position in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an army officer. His rank was captain. it was just before the second war break. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we lived in Leningrad. Because of his sickness, we were sent from Leningrad south in [NON-ENGLISH], which is Krasnodar. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: The real position-- I just say army officer because I don't know what his position was in Army, OK? I know his rank, and I know where he worked, and that's about all. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in the front lines, or in administration?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in administration-- [NON-ENGLISH] administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home that you lived in, the last one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you want me right before the war or just from the beginning?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your home when you were a child. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: What home? My childhood was very good because of my father having position in army. And I don't know whether you know. In those days, army officers and army families was in the first league-- least in first place. Everything was done for the army officers, army people, and of course, their families.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] childhood, particularly, was good because we had the opportunity to not only to go to school and to educate ourselves. But because we belong to sort of house of Red Army, there was a lot of sport activities, dances, singing, and all sort of things, which is for nothing. And of course, my parents gave us all what we can do to make us enjoy our childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not only me, but just as much my sisters. They enjoy all these things. Oh, we were in orchestra. We were in dancing. We were in all sort of sports. We have a basketball team. We have a family basketball team that was three-- two families-- three of us and a family Kowalski. We had first place in Krasnodar. And then in all district Krasnodar. We have not only basketball-- volleyball-- the same thing, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many?

speaker: interviewee
text: So to tell you the truth, in my childhood my parents gave us some much that all would come later on helped me to get through, because later on I have very bad memories. And I thank my parents for all the things they they-- well, they were able, and they give us. Not everybody had this in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] There was many. In Army, I wouldn't say there was many. But my father was very likable. They love him. And we didn't feel much as a Jewish. I have to tell you, we knew we're Jewish. We were in passport written Jewish. And my-- except that we couldn't have old traditions, Jewish tradition, in our family. But we were lucky, because we have grandparents, which were traditional. And they give us a lot of knowledge about Jewish tradition. We knew Pesach. We knew Hanukkah. We knew Purim. We knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rosh Hashanah. We knew all about these because we lived with my mother's mother, who had-- who had seven children. And she-- by herself, because her husband died very early.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and she lived opposite where we lived. It was in Vinnitsa. And Hanukkah gelt-- I remember like today. When Hanukkah come, she was running around to give all her grandchildren and grand-grandchildren money, because that was tradition. When Purim come, she was backing baking hamentashen, and she brought home. I remember for Pesach, we had to go to my father's family, because his father was very traditional. And they used to bake matzos. So I remember like today from matzos, they have to do the matzo meal. So he was standing and crushing this matzo to give us matzo meal, so that the way have [NON-ENGLISH] and all those things. So that's about all what we could have in Jewish tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Russia. We didn't have any-- like, Jewish organization, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, my-- my father was in party-- communist party. We youngsters-- there was a youth-- a youth organization [INAUDIBLE]. We belonged there too. We were very patriotic. We didn't have much to complain about, because when it was very bad in '33-- I remember like today-- we had everything because my father was in army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I attend primary school. I attend high school. And I had two years university.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of ideology were these schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You have to know Marxism. You have to know the line of the party-- Communist party. That was in all schools and universities. You have to learn, like, history. I was a history teacher. I have to learn history. But I have to have history of Russia, history of communism, history of this. That's how it was. And-- and then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it didn't bother us, because we born to think about a day, to live about-- like this, and it was OK with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were at the University for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- I was-- I was doing-- [SIGHS] that's a story. You see, I finished my high school in 1940. To get into university in 1940-- yes, 1940-- to get into university then, they didn't have exams. The best sort of-- how you call it-- that is-- when you get from high school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Graduation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Graduation-- the best graduate people were taking university. With me was this-- I wasn't a bad student. I rather was a good student. But when my exams finished, I had one three, which is no good. Four or five was good. But three to have-- that's bad. So I couldn't get to university.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did in the first year when-- '41-- I have to go to university, I decided if they don't take me, so what I'd do-- I do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] assistant-- medical assistant course. And I put my name. And I was-- but in the 1st September-- no 5th of September-- the 1st September start the university. On 5th of September, I come home. Mom said, you got a letter from university. I said, how come?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took university. And it was for physical education department I had. Because of my sport and being known in Krasnodar all over, they said that we will take you at university in the course you wanted. But you have to come from this sport club-- sport organization, which was [NON-ENGLISH]. That's House of Red Army-- from that to teacher, because that was a teacher in university. And I, of course, said OK. And that's how I from the first-- from about 10 days of university, I went to university.

speaker: interviewer
text: In for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years. Now, '41 and '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home? You see, I never-- we could understand a bit of Yiddish, when Mom and Dad would speak. They didn't speak much, but if they wanted so that we don't know, the children, so they spoke between them Yiddish. We, of course, never learned and never knew. So that was only Russian and Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: We learned Ukrainian because you were living a few years in Ukraine. So we learned this. That's the two languages we knew. But at school, we learned German. So even if I had three-- I was pretty good in German, OK? What did you know about Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, you see, we didn't know much, because in 1939, Hitler signed the contract that he will never invade Russian. And all of a sudden, in June 1941, he invade Ukraine, which is a part of Russia, and start to bomb and all sort of thing. Of course, the Russian people didn't like that. And me, of course not. We didn't know what's going on till later on what Hitler does to people, what he do with people-- doing. So we learn only later. And that help us to survive-- me, my mother. My father was there the first day of the war. He was in the army, and was in the front. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- Had you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you experienced any anti-Semitism in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, as I said, anti-Semitism always exists, more in Ukraine than in center Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say that we, as a family, did sort of see it every time we would all go wherever we are. Because? We were mostly with army people, army families. And at school, we didn't feel like we are Jews. We didn't hear it. In Leningrad-- nothing. So it wasn't as bad. We learn about more later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe because we were young, we didn't play attention to the sort of things. We-- maybe that's why we don't remember this all. But to tell you the truth, that's the only thing that I wouldn't say was so bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did know about the war before 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Not much. I now that my-- my father was in the army when they went to [NON-ENGLISH]. and this was taking. So he was at this campaign. Then some part of Poland went to Russia. That much we knew. But of course, what the German-- the Hitler is no good. We never trust him. He can't be trusted, that he is cruel. But we never learn that he is so cruel, so bad, absolutely not, because Russia wanted to live in peace with Germany. They asked him, and they signed it. So that was the end. Unless it started in '41. And then when they took Ukraine, when people start to run from there to our places, where we were, further up, they were telling what's going on. That's when we learn who is Hitler, what he does to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh that, first of all, he's a-- he's against Jews. All Jews were killed in Ukraine as soon as he came, yes? And that we have to be alert, that he send them-- a lot of people sent from Ukraine to Germany for work. That's all what they said. And they run. My husband was the first to run from Poland to Russia. That's how he stayed alive. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't change anything between 1941 and the time that you were invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Change? Look, I was-- I-- I was a patriot, and I wanted to go to army. But because I was the eldest-- I have a call to army. But because I was the eldest, my mother went there and said, no, she is the eldest. My husband now is fighting. I can't lose my husband and my eldest daughter. And that's how I stayed with the family. But when-- we are supposed to be evacuated as soon the army-- Hitler-- come closer to the place we lived. I mean Krasnodar. We were-- I was calling on them and ringing them to do something about it, that we are Jewish. We live in army blocks. Everybody knows us. You should evacuate. We would hear that they're already in [NON-ENGLISH]. They're going very close. But they said, wait. When the time come, we will evacuated. So we wait. And of course, when [INAUDIBLE] evacuate, it was in 1942. So we didn't went too far. About 200 kilometers or 250 kilometers from Krasnodar, we were surrounded. And not only we, but hundreds of people, thousands of people, hundreds of wagons or by train was stopped. And [INAUDIBLE]-- they bombed them all. And we were surrounded by German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened, just going back to August '42, when you were evacuated. What actually happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- they come and said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was 'they'?

speaker: interviewee
text: --now, you-- you have-- that's army officials come and said, now, you have to get ready tomorrow or day after tomorrow. I don't remember how they said, but it was very short notice that you have to pack and be ready to evacuate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were ready. We didn't pack much. We just left everything that we had. My father-- we had a grand piano, which my father bought us that we can learn piano. We left that. He sold his suit to get us a piano. And we all played piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left everything behind. We took a few cases-- suitcases-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and they come-- they come-- no, no, they come by trucks. They put us on the trucks, and we went to the tram-- trains. They put us in wagons. Of course, normal wagons--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: --were sitting-- sitting. It's not like cattle wagons. It was normal trains. And we-- we had our [INAUDIBLE] that was four people. And they took us. We went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we're going [LAUGHS] until they start bomb. The fire in the beginning is right there. They stopped all the trains. And the people ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was approximately in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia. It's not far-- as I said, it was about 250 kilometers, 300 at the most kilometers, from Krasnodar. [INAUDIBLE], they start bombed us. They surrendered the whole thing. And there we are in the middle of nowhere with nothing. And it's not only we-- thousands, thousand people run from this. Some of them died because they-- the bomb went on their wagons. And if they were not quick enough to get out of wagons, particularly the front one, they all died. Nobody left. But we were lucky, because you were in-- in sort of not maybe in the middle, further of the middle of this whole thing. So we survived. And with us a lot of people survived. I remember like this. A lot of people start runnin'. And most of them went right. And I said, Mom, we're going left, because the bombs were still there. And we went left. And we walk and walk and walk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --till we come-- huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, my mother-- my two sisters, my mother, and me. We walk. And we come [DRINKS] to-- what was that place called? [RUSSIAN] or something. Maybe we should stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying approximately where you were fleeing to. You were describing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were fleeing to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere about-- we walked about 50, 60 kilometers-- we come to a club. I remember club was written. We went to this club-- not only we, a few families. It was a big room and we stayed there. And I remember we still didn't see Germans. We saw the bombs and things like this. But we didn't see Germans. We were staying there about two days or three. On the third day, somebody said, Germans are here. And yes, they come. And they come to the club. And I don't want even to talk about this, [SOBBING] because from then it started. Germans come to my sister. They want to take her. Then they come to me. My mother start to scream. We're all screaming and crying. The whole club-- [INAUDIBLE] club because they start shooting. And we thought, oh, that's it. That's finished. I didn't know what happened. I can't remember, but I walked out after the screaming and things like this. My-- my mother-- like, I-- I-- I-- was like mad. Mad-- I was running like idiot-- my mother decide-- and screaming. So-- so-- they run out. But we didn't-- we stayed overnight. In the morning, we start run. We went further and further. And we come-- OK. I can [INAUDIBLE] speak. I can speak, OK. And then we went further and further. And we come to a one that was Stanitsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, because it's still where we-- w not that far from the place we live-- we left-- Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is still August, 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's already goes to next month-- September. I'm going to talk about August, September, October. That's-- you see, that's the whole period that we already were in German occupation. We were already saw them. We saw the-- the German in August. But since-- from there on, there were-- we didn't see them often. But we knew they're there. And we try to avoid them. So we walked.
